name: Designer Tribute Website - IKEA
about: As one of our many assignments of sophomore year's web design class, we were to create a designer tribute for an important person we look up to most. Being one who loves IKEA, walking around each and every one of its corridors simply for the sake of it, I decided to make a tribute for the creator of IKEA.
para1: Knowing the basics of Bootstrap and effectively applying it to our website was essential. Of course, adding a navbar to my website was a must. From getbootstrap.com, I incorporated a responsive navbar, allowing the user to navigate to other pages of my tribute website. I even utilized the dropdown menu, adding some details about IKEA; after all, nobody deserves to miss out on some IKEA food.
para2: Primarily using assets from getbootstrap.com, I utilized carousels to add some style to my website. This piece of code was merely copy-and-paste just like the navbar, but I still learned its mechanics over time due to enough familiarity with incorporating them into my website and adding my own data into it.
para3: Speaking of carousels, I came up with the idea of putting carousels inside of bootstrap cards. At the time, I thought of such an idea to be a unique twist on another one of my ideas where I put cards inside of carousels, forgetting the fact that shopping websites had both ideas since forever. Nevertheless, I incorporated it into the 'current designs' page. One confusing part of having multiple carousels in one page was to figure out how to get each carousel to slide to the next slide without other carousels simultaneously doing the same. Eventually, I figured it out; I simply had to give each carousel a different ID!
reflection: Overall, creating the website was a fun process. I would say, though, that some parts were pretty tedious. From researching Ingvar Kamprad, hard-coding each carousel, and manually creating multiple of the same element, I may have grown exhausted from using Ctrl+C and Ctrl+V millions of times, but I still grew exposure to advanced mechanics of code via Bootstrap and learned from it. Looking back at this website, I could definitely have created it faster with the knowledge I have now: Vue, JSON files, etc. However, I created a great website just as well without that knowledge.

name: Karen Coffee Shop Website
about: Based on actual Karen Cafes, I came up with the idea of making a coffee shop website Karen Cafe - themed. With my group, I helped create a humorous yet aesthetically pleasing website with pink and gold everywhere. The website even includes a dedicated page to ridiculous rules!
para1: For this website, I wanted to test out adding images as my navbar brand. For that, one of my groupmates crafted a lovely logo that accurately resembles a Karen, consisting of a lady's head with the signature Karen bob and some bubblegum-blowing to signify her attitude. That, indeed, became the navbar brand image. However, I had some difficulty with determining the right size. If it were a few pixels too big, the entire navbar would seem too tall, and vice versa. Eventually, I determined the right size to be 80x80 pixels, ensuring the image wouldn't look stretched out or look too big/small. Not only did I want a navbar brand, but I also wanted the navbar to have a glittery background that faded on the bottom, instead of using the usual Bootstrap colors or turning it to some shade of pink. It was quite a hassle finding the right image, since some images either didn't actually have a transparent background or cut off too rigidly on the bottom of the navbar, when I wanted a smooth cutoff. Eventually, my group and I resolved to using a golden glitter background and decided to ignore its slightly-abrupt cutoff. It was the best we could do.
para2: In order to incorporate some sass into our website, I thought of making a hover effect in the 'About Us' section: on hover, the word 'kind' would be replaced by 'mean', which would be crossed-out looking and colored red. After significant research on how to do  it, my teacher and I noted that the effect would need JavaScript. Since our group was already running low on time and had to submit the project soon, I decided to make the word 'mean' appear next to the word 'kind' via CSS and turning the word 'kind' into its own 'p' element inside of the 'p' element encompassing the entire 'About Us' paragraph, since I couldn't make the word 'kind' disappear.
para3: Since I saw other classmates have forms on past websites, my group and I decided on adding a contact form on this website. After all, what is a restaurant website without a contact form? To do that, I copied and pasted a Bootstrap form template from getbootstrap.com, of course. Then came the real deal: decorating the form. Surely, we decorated most of the form. However, in order to edit the actual input boxes and buttons, we had to find a way to access them. For the button, I wanted to add a magenta color as its background, since the goal of styling the entire website was to make it as pink as possible. One of my groupmates worked on setting the margins, so the contact form still looks flawless even on smaller screens. It took some trial and error, but we got it to work in the end.
reflection: After every project, our teacher would spin a wheel with everybody's names on it, with the chosen person having to present their website to the class. Our group was selected in one of the rounds, and our website was shown to the entire class. I'm proud to say that it got a few chuckles out of the room, as intended. Realizing our creativity, our group felt honored to be sharing this great project to the class, and what I can take away from this experience is that I should keep up the creative flow in my future projects.

name: Serenescope Article Gallery
about: Our monthly website project for the month of April was a 'wildcard website', where we could make any website we wanted. I decided to make an article gallery, feeling determined to put in the immense amount of effort it takes to make one manually. The gallery consists of 12 total articles, with 3 belonging to each of 4 categories: Economics, STEM, Pop Culture, and Environment. Each article has its own dedicated website.
para1:
Every article gallery has a 'Search Articles' feature. It's to no surprise that mine has one as well. However, my 'Search Articles' feature is a more basic build, containing no advanced search algorithms that mostly exist in modern applications and websites. Still, I took building that basic version as an opportunity to explore how a search feature works at its core and to further practice building and understanding JavaScript logic. In order to successfully make this feature, I first created an array full of strings of all 12 articles' names. After that, I added an event listener to the button next to my input field, attaching a function that finds the closest-matching article(s) to what the user types in. In the function, if the user did type something, then a for-loop will search through my array filled with the 12 articles' names for the string that matches the user's input. If it does, a list element is created with classes that visually align it, filled with the found match from the loop, then appended inside of the '
    ' HTML code containing the class 'searchStuff'.
para2: Coming up with article ideas was the fun part. Actually writing each article? Not so much. By the end of spring break, my Google Document was filled with information and research about 12 different things. Even though the process was quite tedious, I saw this as an opportunity to improve my writing skills. After that, it was merely copy-and-paste and manually typing code. Eventually, each piece of my website was coming together, until I had all 12 articles beautifully framed.
reflection: Overall, this website was a tedious yet rewarding process for me. I learned how the search feature works by creating a basic version of that algorithm, and I diligently did my research on 12 topics, which improved my writing skills. If my current self were to make this website, I would have come in with much more knowledge of efficiency. That is, using Vue to automate the tedious parts of coding, such as the rows of cards pertaining to each article, a simpler way to code the 'More Articles To Read' section, and a better strategy in building the logic of the search feature. Still, what I did back then was impressive enough, and I hope to keep doing more impressive projects in the future.

name: Little Bird Toy Company Website
about: As the last project of junior year web design, my partner and I created a website based on a fictional toy company. The name, logos, information, and images of the company were given by our teacher, and it was our duty to make a website about the company and its products using what we had learned in JavaScript.
para1: For this project, all groups were required to create a landing page. Thinking about animated first-person video games' home screens, I decided to go for an animated landing page. Using my CSS skills, I succeeded in making the little bird slide into view, with the wordy version of the logo appearing next to the bird's final position after, and finally, the 3 buttons that allow the user to navigate to the other pages of our website.
para2: What is a company website without displayed reviews? In order to make multiple review cards appear without manually coding each one, I created a separate JavaScript file consisting of an array of objects. That array is called 'reviews', and it stores a bunch of objects with properties such as 'firstName', 'lastName', 'age', etc. The function in this image is housed in a different JavaScript file, and it renders all objects of that 'reviews' array by accessing an HTML element called 'grid', then appending card elements for each item in the 'reviews' array using a for-each loop.
para3: When it came to rendering multiple products of this fictional company, I was initially planning on making simple product cards similar to how I did the review cards. However, a sudden idea dawned upon me that I should put a carousel in each of the cards, showing multiple images of each product instead of just one. Initially, I didn't quite know how to do this. Realizing that each modal and carousel had to have unique IDs per card and trying to make the IDs unique while keeping track of which ID belongs to which element especially confused me. Another problem was that I didn't know how to go about building logic that determined how many slides the carousel should have for each product card, since each of the products had an unequal amount of images. I couldn't figure out what to do when some cards had unused, blank carousel slides. Since I didn't have enough knowledge to erase the extra carousel slides for the cards that didn't need them, I decided to fill the rest of the shorter 'images' arrays of each product with a 'No image availale' image in order to make all products have an equal amount of images, thus making use of the unused carousel slides for some of the product cards in some form.
reflection: I found making this website to be challenging, yet satisfying. The challenging parts of the website were primarily making the product-cards' and review-cards' logic work after tons of trial and error. The satisfying parts were making the page stylishly come together and the relief of not having to manually code each review card and product card, given how much code the product card would've taken, especially due to creating modals and carousels within modals.

name: About Famous Person Website - Queen Mary I
Team Players Website - Blair Witch Project
Course Planner Website
about: Marlboro High School students have a hard time with picking the right classes that fit them, especially when planning adequate course rigor for colleges and passing graduation requirements. This especially happens between January to March. Created with a partner, this Course Planner website is meant to provide course schedule options a student might want to take based on the courses they already took, their grade, their leftover graduation requirements, and their career plans after Marlboro High School. This website is meant to be taken as inspiration/information for the next classes the student might take.
para1: Using Vue to render multiple clickable options was quite challenging. Not only that, but my school had tons of options for every subject, which meant much tedious work in creating a separate JSON file. In my JSON file, every object had a property called 'subject', in which I put the subject the course object belonged to. Making the file took several hours, but I eventually got it done. In fact, this was the longest JSON file I've ever written; around 122 total objects were manually written by me.
para2: Moving along, my partner and I had to find a way to create the algorithm for recommending the user courses based on their picked major and current courses taken. In doing so, my partner first had to code a counter of how many courses for each subject that the user had selected. Then, we had to filter the gigantic JSON file array, finding the objects that weren't the user's already-taken courses and included the user's selected major. Then, we rendered a list of all found courses. The image here shows a glimpse of our code that returns a filtered array full of courses belonging to the subject the method header is labeled with.
para3: Of course, I had to include a bonus feature: the favorites page! This is one of my first projects where I applied my knowledge of localStorage. To do so, I first pushed whatever the user marked as 'favorite' into a 'favorites' array. Then, I turned that array into a string using a special method called 'Stringify()', and then set it as an item in localStorage, a built-in temporary storage space. From a different JavaScript file, I accessed that item through local storage, then displayed it using classic Vue techniques.
reflection: I found creating this website to be a fun experience, especially while working with a partner. My partner was a significant help in the creation of this planner, finding better strategies for building the logic. I primarily focused on building logic for the 'favorites' page and styling the website, and my partner primarily focused on building the logic of rendering and recommending courses. After presenting our final product to the classroom, we were met with lots of positive feedback on the style and idea of the website. One thing we could improve on was to take into account the instances where some courses our website didn't allow to have been taken together should be allowed to have been taken together. Still, my partner and I had an excellent idea that successfully came to life.

name: Inner Light Pathways Website
about: This client project was created for my yoga teacher, who recently started her own yoga studio as of May 2025. Thinking of this project as an improved version of her old website, and something to advertise her business with, I decided to make one just for her. This website includes information about her, offered classes, pricing, and a contact form that actually sends her emails.
para1: As any official business's website would have reviews, I thought of rendering 8 5-star reviews I found from Google as cards in a carousel, except this time I would use Vue to render them. Up to this point, I manually did so. But this time, I wanted to try it out the Vue way. At first, getting the carousel to show only 3 cards per slide was challenging. All 8 cards appeared on one slide, overlapping each other. To solve this problem, I created a function where a for-loop returned an array of 3 reviews per call of the function, and I rendered the cards for every slide through that function.
para2: My yoga teacher's old website had accordions for the Q&A sections, and I wanted to add the same kind of feature in this new website as well. So, I filled a JSON file with questions from the old website, and used Microsoft Copilot to extract the answers from the website. Unfortunately, the AI could not actually extract the data even though the old website is public, and instead paraphrased the answers. Feeling unsatisfied, I manually had to copy and paste each answer, one by one, in the json file. Running the website, I noticed that the answers in the accordions seemed too 'bland'. To double-check, I went back to the old website and saw that she had bullet points, tables, subheadings, and bolded words. After tons of thinking, I realized that I had to manually place html elements within the 'answer' string in the json file, and make sure the string is all in one line. It definitely required some spatial reasoning. After 2-3 hours, I had finally completed the file. Then, when I ran the website, the answers in the accordions were much more similar in format to the old website's, and were less bland to look at.
reflection: I presented the final product both to my class and to my yoga teacher. The class really liked this website, but suggested that I make the sections on the home page for 'Reiki', 'Yoga', and 'Sound' to be taller, so there is less of a white gap in the background to look at. Another area of improvement for this website would be having less words, since the purpose of a website is to provide more visual appeal than information. My yoga teacher really liked this website, which also has one of her domains added to it.
